message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

Check this wording from README.md; it isn't terribly correct.  It uses "Nameplate" in places that should be crucible.  What do you think?  Read RBS0 for context.


Charge — Start the Sentry/Pentacle/Bottle triad for a Nameplate. Charging brings up the Crucible in dependency order: Pentacle creates the namespace, Sentry configures policy, then the Bottle starts with its network already constrained.

Quench — Stop and clean up a Charged Nameplate's containers. Quenching tears down the Crucible in reverse order and removes the network resources created during Charging.

Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content: 369 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 370 371 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 372 373 ### Lifecycle 374 375 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 376 [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 377 378 **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. 379 [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 380 381 ### Interaction 382 383 **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. 384 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 385 386 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 387 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. 388 389 **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 390 [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 391 392 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 393 394 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 395 396 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. 397 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. 398 Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 399 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). 400 The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. 401 Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 402 403 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification.

file.content: ## Appendix: Crucible Operations Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. ### Lifecycle **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). ### Interaction **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification.

content.text:
You're right on both, and RBS0 backs it up — a Nameplate is configuration, a Crucible is the running triad. You Charge/Quench a Crucible, not a Nameplate. The rest of your README already uses this cleanly (line 213: "Charge the Crucible for a Nameplate to start the Sentry…"; line 215: "Quench the Crucible to stop and clean up…"). The appendix entries at README.md:375-379 drifted.

Two issues, not one:

**Charge line** — "triad for a Nameplate" is defensible (the triad is configured *per* Nameplate) but drops the term being defined nearby. The triad **is** the Crucible — so naming it there tightens the definition.

**Quench line** — "a Charged Nameplate's containers" is the actual error. A Nameplate isn't Charged; a Crucible is.

Proposed rewrite:

```
Charge — Start a Crucible — the Sentry/Pentacle/Bottle triad — for a Nameplate.
Charging brings up the Crucible in dependency order: Pentacle creates the
namespace, Sentry configures policy, then the Bottle starts with its network
already constrained.

Quench — Stop and clean up a Charged Crucible's containers.
Quenching tears down the Crucible in reverse order and removes the network
resources created during Charging.
```

The Charge rewrite keeps the Nameplate reference (it's still what parameterizes the Crucible) but makes "Crucible" the noun being started. The Quench rewrite is the straight correction.

Want me to apply this edit?

content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) builds and supply-chain [provenance](#Provenance) 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation chain, [build isolation](#BuildIsolation), and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. 16 > Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 17 18 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 19 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 20 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 21 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 22 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 23 24 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 25 26

27 28

29 30 ## Environment 31 32 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable [provenance](#Provenance), and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 33 The two compose but neither requires the other. 34 35 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 36 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 37 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 38 39 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 40 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 41 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 42 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 43 44 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 45 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 46 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 47 48 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 49 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 50 51 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. 52 Commands that produce artifacts write them here. 53 Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 54 55 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 56 57 ``` 58 tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh 59 ``` 60 61 ## Foundry 62 63 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 64 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 65 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) ([egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) build from source with [SLSA provenance](#Provenance)), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 66 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 67 68 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 69 Builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 70 71 ### Depot 72 73 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 74 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 75 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 76 77 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 78 79 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. 80 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. 81 [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. 82 Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. 83 See [Build Isolation](#BuildIsolation) for the security rationale behind these profiles. 84 85 ### Manor 86 87 The [Payor's](#Payor) administrative seat — holds the billing account, OAuth client, and operator identity. 88 [Depot](#Depot) projects are created and funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) authority. 89 The [Manor](#Manor) has its own GCP project, distinct from any [Depot](#Depot) project. 90 91 ### Payor 92 93 [Establishes](#Establish) a [Manor](#Manor) and funds [Depot](#Depot) projects through it; authenticates via OAuth. 94 The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — [Establishing](#Establish) the [Manor](#Manor), configuring OAuth, and [Installing](#Install) credentials via browser flow. 95 All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 96 97 ### Governor 98 99 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. 100 The [Governor](#Governor) is [Mantled](#Mantle) by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). 101 The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) for build access, [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever) for pull access, and [Forfeits](#Forfeit) credentials when they are no longer needed. 102 103 ### Director 104 105 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). 106 Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 107 The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a build, [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Rekon](#Rekon) raw tags, [Vouch](#Vouch) [provenance](#Provenance), [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. 108 109 ### Retriever 110 111 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). 112 This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 113 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use, [Plumbs](#Plumb) their [provenance](#Provenance), or [Wrests](#Wrest) a specific image directly. 114 115 ### Vessel 116 117 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). 118 Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. 119 A fourth mode, [Kludge](#Kludge), builds locally for development without involving the [Depot](#Depot). 120 121 ### Hallmark 122 123 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. 124 [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of [provenance](#Provenance) tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. 125 Each [Hallmark](#Hallmark) produces three tagged artifacts in the [Depot](#Depot) registry: the container image (`-image`), the [software bill of materials](#SBOM) ([`-about`](#About)), and the cryptographic attestation ([`-vouch`](#Vouch)). 126 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files to pin a [Crucible](#Crucible) to specific image versions. 127 128 ### Foundry Lifecycle 129 130 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 131 132 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 133 |------|-------------------|---------| 134 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 135 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 136 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies [provenance](#Provenance) | 137 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 138 139 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. 140 All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 141 142 #### Establishment and Provisioning 143 144 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by [Establishing](#Establish) a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. 145 After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) [Installs](#Install) them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 146 147 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. 148 The [Payor](#Payor) then [Mantles](#Mantle) a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 149 150 Before the first build can run, the [Depot](#Depot) needs its supply-chain infrastructure in place: upstream base images must be [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the registry, and a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) of builder tool images must be inscribed. 151 152 #### Credential Distribution 153 154 The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. 155 Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 156 157 #### Build and Retrieve 158 159 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. 160 After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their [provenance](#Provenance). 161 [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 162 163 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 164 165 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 166 167 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 168 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 169 - [SLSA provenance](#Provenance) attestation and verification 170 - [Software Bills of Material (SBOM)](#SBOM) for every build 171 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 172 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 173 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 174 175 Each build's source context is packaged as a [Pouch](#Pouch) — the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 176 177 ## Crucible 178 179 The distinctive case [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain [provenance](#Provenance). 180 Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. 181 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy — without requiring modifications to existing container images. 182 The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 183 184 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 185 The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. 186 [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 187 188 ### Nameplate 189 190 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). 191 The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. 192 Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 193 194 ### Containers 195 196 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. 197 The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). 198 A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 199 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). 200 The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. 201 Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 202 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. 203 The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). 204 Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 205 206 ### Enclave 207 208 The isolated network connecting a [Bottle](#Bottle) to its [Sentry](#Sentry) — the [Bottle's](#Bottle) only path to the outside world. 209 All [Bottle](#Bottle) traffic routes through the [Enclave](#Enclave) to the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway; the [Bottle](#Bottle) has no interface on any other network. 210 211 ### Crucible Lifecycle 212 213 [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) to start the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 214 [Rack](#Rack) the [Bottle](#Bottle) to shell in, [Hail](#Hail) the [Sentry](#Sentry) to inspect the gateway, or [Scry](#Scry) the network to observe traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 215 When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 216 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its [provenance](#Provenance) — the full view shows the [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 217 218 ### Reference Nameplates 219 220 Shipped [Nameplates](#Nameplate) demonstrating different [Crucible](#Crucible) configurations. 221 Each pairs a [Sentry](#Sentry) with a [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) and defines the network policy for that deployment target. 222 223 Anthropic — SSH entry from the workstation, OAuth authentication via copy/paste, everything else blocked. 225 [Kludge](#Kludge)-only development target: no cloud account, no service account credentials, fully self-contained on the developer's workstation. 226 The onboarding handbook's first hands-on track teaches the full [Crucible](#Crucible) lifecycle using [ccyolo](#ccyolo). 227 228 **[tadmor](#tadmor)** — Adversarial security testing [Crucible](#Crucible) for daily iteration. 229 The [tadmor](#tadmor) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under a restrictive network allowlist, consuming [Kludged](#Kludge) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for fast author-test-iterate cycles. 230 The [Theurge](#Theurge) test orchestrator [Charges](#Charge) [tadmor](#tadmor) and dispatches curated escape attempts to validate that the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment holds under adversarial conditions. 231 232 **[moriah](#moriah)** — Adversarial security testing [Crucible](#Crucible) for the airgap supply chain. 233 The [moriah](#moriah) [Nameplate](#Nameplate) pairs the [Sentry](#Sentry) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) attack [Vessel](#Vessel) under the same restrictive network allowlist as [tadmor](#tadmor), consuming [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) [Ordained](#Ordain) end-to-end on the [Airgap](#Airgap) pool. 234 The produces the inputs. 235 236 ## Appendix: Foundry Operations 237 238 Formal definitions for all [Foundry](#Foundry) operations, organized by lifecycle phase. 239 240 ### Infrastructure 241 242 [Establishing](#Establish) walks the [Payor](#Payor) through project creation, API enablement, and consent screen configuration — the manual prerequisites before any automated operations can run. 244 consumption, and any throttling in effect for the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project. 263 264 ### Credentials 265 266 **[Mantle](#Mantle)** — Create or replace the [Governor](#Governor) service account for a [Depot](#Depot). 267 [Mantling](#Mantle) is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that provisions the administrative credential — the [Governor](#Governor) inherits the [Payor's](#Payor) authority to manage the [Depot](#Depot) but authenticates via service account key rather than OAuth. 268 269 **[Knight](#Knight)** — Create a [Director](#Director) service account. 270 [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 271 272 **[Charter](#Charter)** — Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account. 273 [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 274 275 **[Forfeit](#Forfeit)** — Revoke a service account credential. 276 [Forfeiting](#Forfeit) deletes the longer needed, or being rotated. 278 279 **[List Service Accounts](#ListSAs)** — Inventory all service accounts issued within a [Depot](#Depot). 280 Shows [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), registry. 285 [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 286 287 **[Reliquary](#Reliquary)** — Co-versioned set of builder tool images (skopeo, docker, gcloud, syft) inscribed from upstream into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 288 Cloud Build jobs use [Reliquary](#Reliquary) images as step containers, ensuring builds run with known, project-owned toolchains rather than pulling tools from upstream at build time. 289 The [Director](#Director) inscribes a [Reliquary](#Reliquary) before any [Ordain](#Ordain) or [Enshrine](#Enshrine) operation can run. 290 291 ### Building 292 293 **[Ordain](#Ordain)** — Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. 294 [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. 295 Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the [Depot](#Depot) registry with associated [provenance](#Provenance) metadata. 296 297 **[Conjure](#Conjure)** — Cloud Build creates the image from source. 298 [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an [egress-locked](#BuildIsolation) environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full [SLSA](#Provenance) attestation and [SBOMs](#SBOM). 299 This is the highest-trust build mode. 300 301 **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 302 [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 303 Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build [provenance](#Provenance). 304 305 **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 306 [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 307 This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 308 309 **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. 310 [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 311 The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 312 313 **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. 314 The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 315 This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 316 317 ### Verification 318 319 **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) confirm build completion. 322 323 **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. 324 [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. 325 Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag Inspect an artifact's [provenance](#Provenance) — [SBOM](#SBOM), build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. 336 [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. 337 Two views are available: full ([SBOM](#SBOM), build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 338 339 ### Distribution 340 341 **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). 368 369 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 370 371 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 372 373 ### Lifecycle 374 375 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start a [Crucible](#Crucible) — the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad — defined by a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 376 container. 384 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 385 386 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 387 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 391 392 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 393 394 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 395 396 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. 397 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. 398 Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 399 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). 400 The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. 401 Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 402 403 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. 404 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. 405 Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. 406 The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. 407 408 ## Appendix: Supply Chain Provenance 409 410 Supply chain provenance is a cryptographically signed record of how a container image was produced — what source, what builder, what steps — so that consumers can verify an image came from trusted infrastructure and was not tampered with in transit or at rest. 411 412 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) achieves [SLSA](https://slsa.dev) v1.0 Build Level 3 for [Conjure](#Conjure) builds, auto-generated by Google Cloud Build. 413 The [Vouch](#Vouch) step independently verifies each build's DSSE envelope signature against Google's attestor public keys from `projects/verified-builder` KMS — using Python standard library and `openssl` only, with no third-party verifier. 414 415 Provenance guarantees are mode-aware: 416 417 | [Vessel](#Vessel) Mode | Trust Basis | [Vouch](#Vouch) Verdict | 418 |------|-------------|------| 419 | [**Conjure**](#Conjure) | Full SLSA v1.0 Level 3 — signed build provenance from GCB | DSSE envelope signature verification | 420 | [**Bind**](#Bind) | Digest-pin comparison — image in GAR matches pinned upstream reference | Digest-pin match | 421 | [**Graft**](#Graft) | Locally built and pushed — no cloud build involvement | GRAFTED (explicit no-provenance marker) | 422 423 Deliberately excluded: no `slsa-verifier` binary, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation, no `jq` in the verification path. 424 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verifier reconstructs Pre-Authenticated Encoding (PAE), decodes the base64url payload and signature, and verifies via `openssl dgst` against embedded attestor keys — a minimal, auditable trust chain. 425 426 ## Appendix: Software Bill of Materials 427 428 A Software Bill of Materials ([SBOM](#SBOM)) is a machine-readable inventory of every component inside a container image — every OS package, every library, every binary, with versions. 429 Without one, a container image is an opaque filesystem whose contents you discover by running it, which is exactly the wrong time to find out it ships a vulnerable dependency. 430 431 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) generates an [SBOM](#SBOM) for every build using [Syft](https://github.com/anchore/syft), scanning each per-platform image during the [About](#About) assembly step. 432 Each architecture gets its own [SBOM](#SBOM), bundled alongside the build transcript and configuration snapshot in the `-about` artifact stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 433 434 An [SBOM](#SBOM) enables three hygiene practices that opaque images cannot support: 435 436 - **CVE triage before deployment** — when a vulnerability is announced, search your [SBOMs](#SBOM) rather than scanning running containers 437 - **Pre-deployment audit** — know what you are granting network access to before a [Crucible](#Crucible) is [Charged](#Charge) 438 - **Build-over-build drift detection** — compare [SBOMs](#SBOM) across [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to see what changed between builds 439 440 The [Plumb](#Plumb) command surfaces [SBOM](#SBOM) contents: the full view shows package inventories; the compact view summarizes key components. 441 442 ## Appendix: Build Isolation 443 444 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) supports two build egress profiles — [Tethered](#Tethered) and [Airgap](#Airgap) — that determine whether a Cloud Build job can reach the public internet. 445 The distinction is not primarily about availability; it is a security boundary that controls what can enter and exit the build environment. 446 447 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) protects: exfiltration and supply chain injection.** 448 If a compromised dependency, build plugin, or Dockerfile instruction executes during your build, an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build cannot phone home — it cannot transmit source code, secrets, or intermediate artifacts to an external endpoint, and it cannot silently fetch malicious payloads. 449 This is defense-in-depth for proprietary code: even if a build step is compromised, the network is not available as an exfiltration channel. 450 451 **The curated gate principle.** 452 [Airgap](#Airgap) does not mean "nothing external." It means all external content enters through a single auditable gate — the [Enshrine](#Enshrine) ceremony — rather than ad-hoc network fetches during build. 453 The attack surface collapses from "any URL a Dockerfile mentions" to "the specific digests the [Director](#Director) [Enshrined](#Enshrine)." 454 Builder tool images enter through a parallel gate: the [Reliquary](#Reliquary), inscribed once and pinned by digest for all subsequent builds. 455 456 **What [Airgap](#Airgap) does not protect: the base image contents.** 457 Base images like `debian-slim` were themselves built with full internet access — `apt-get install` already ran inside them. 458 The [Airgap](#Airgap) protects *your* build steps on top of those bases, not the base image contents themselves. 459 Base images are vetted separately: digest-pinned at [Enshrine](#Enshrine) time, inspectable via [SBOM](#SBOM), and stored as project-owned copies in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 460 A [Tethered](#Tethered) build of the base image followed by an [Airgapped](#Airgap) build of your application is the expected pattern — the base image is a known input, your proprietary layers are the protected output. 461 462 **Regulatory alignment.** 463 No framework mandates build-time network blocking by name, but egress-locked builds are the simplest way to evidence several common controls: FedRAMP CM-7 (least functionality) and SC-7 (boundary protection), SOC 2 CC6.1 (logical access) and CC8.1 (change management), and SLSA Level 3's hermetic build requirement. 464 465 ## Appendix: Roadmap 466 467 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 468 469 - **Crucible conduit for cloud services** - Encrypted tunnel from the [Sentry](#Sentry) to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints, enabling [Bottles](#Bottle) to reach cloud AI services (AWS Bedrock, Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI) without exposing floating cloud IP ranges in the CIDR allowlist. 470 WireGuard terminated at the [Sentry](#Sentry) replaces per-service IP tracking with a single stable VPC CIDR. 471 Near-term, allowlist-only [Nameplates](#Nameplate) targeting specific service CIDRs and domains work today with existing [Sentry](#Sentry) machinery. 472 The tunnel adds defense-in-depth for PrivateLink-capable services; SaaS endpoints without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com) remain served by CIDR/domain allowlisting. 473 474 - **Credential confinement** - Move cloud service credentials (API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys) from the operator's workstation into the [Crucible](#Crucible), injected via [Nameplate](#Nameplate) [Regime](#Regime) configuration. 475 The workstation starts the [Crucible](#Crucible) but never holds service credentials — reducing the attack surface from "everything on the workstation" to "one minimal container." 476 Naturally paired with conduit work: credentials and network access are configured together. 477 478 - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. 479 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 480 If a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture, the VPC-SC perimeter should serve both Cloud Build [egress lockdown](#BuildIsolation) and [Bottle](#Bottle) conduit consumers. 481 482 - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. 483 Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 484 485 - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. 486 A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 487 488 - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. 489 On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. 490 Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 491 492 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). 493 The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 494 495 ## Appendix: Reference Project 496 497 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 498 The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. 499 500 | Path | Description | 501 |------|-------------| 502 | `Project Root/` | | 503 | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | 504 | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | 505 | `├── Tools/` | | 506 | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | 507 | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | 508 | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | 509 | `├── identity and build configuration | 512 | `│ ├── ccyolo/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox [Crucible](#Crucible) | 513 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — additional test vessels)` | busybox variants for [Theurge](#Theurge) fixture coverage | 553 554 ## Appendix: Specific Regimes 555 556 **[BURC](#BURC)** — Project structure configuration, in the repo. 557 [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) directory, tools directory. 558 559 **[BURS](#BURS)** — Developer workstation configuration. 560 Not in git. 561 Log directory, station paths. 562 563 **[RBRR](#RBRR)** —
